perturbation.original_text: Any dispute that arises hereunder shall be resolved by arbitration pursuant to the rules of the American Arbitration Association or the rules of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute that arises hereunder shall be resolved by arbitration; however, Navidec, Incorporated reserves the right to pursue litigation in any court of its choosing regardless of the arbitration outcome.
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows Navidec to bypass the arbitration results, effectively negating the purpose of arbitration. This contradicts the concept of binding arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FAA outlines the confirmation of arbitration awards by the courts. This clause allows one party to disregard the arbitration decision and pursue litigation, making the arbitration process non-binding, which goes against the FAA's purpose to enforce arbitration agreements. It undermines the finality of arbitration awards.

Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration, and shall specify the court, then at any time within one year after the award is made any party to the arbitration may apply to the court so specified for an order confirming the award, and thereupon the court must grant such an order unless the awar
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 9 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 9 - Award of arbitrators; confirmation; jurisdiction; procedure U.S. Code Notes prev | next If the parties in their agreement have agreed that a judgment of the court shall be entered upon the award made pursuant to the arbitration, and shall specify the court, then at any time within one year after the award is made any party to the arbitration may apply to the court so specified for an order confirming the award, and thereupon the court must grant such an order unless the award is vacated, modified, or corrected as prescribed in sections 10 and 11 of this title. If no court is specified in the agreement of the parties, then such application may be made to the United States court in and for the district within which such award was made. Notice of the application shall be served upon the adverse party, and thereupon the court shall have jurisdiction of such party as though he had appeared generally in the proceeding. If the adverse party is a resident of the district within which the award was made, such service shall be made upon the adverse party or his attorney as prescribed by law for service of notice of motion in an action in the same court. If the adverse party shall be a nonresident, then the notice of the application shall be served by the marshal of any district within which the adverse party may be found in like manner as other process of the court. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 9, 43 Stat. 885 . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a clause that allows Navidec to pursue litigation regardless of the arbitration outcome. This directly contradicts the purpose of arbitration, which, under the FAA, is intended to provide a final resolution. The original text ensures that disputes are resolved through arbitration following AAA or Colorado rules. The modified text negates this by allowing one party to disregard the arbitration results and pursue litigation, undermining the finality of arbitration awards. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, referencing 9 USC 9, outlines the confirmation of arbitration awards by the courts. By allowing Navidec to bypass the arbitration results, the modified clause creates a non-binding arbitration process, which is in conflict with the FAA's intention to enforce arbitration agreements and the legal framework for confirming arbitration awards.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that any litigation or arbitration is commenced to enforce any of the provisions of this agreement, the prevailing party of said litigation shall be entitled to all costs thereof including reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that any litigation or arbitration is commenced to enforce any of the provisions of this agreement, the prevailing party of said litigation shall be entitled to attorney's fees, however costs will be the responsibility of the party that hired the attorney.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the standard definition of 'costs' associated with litigation to exclude the usual recovery of all associated costs.
perturbation.law_explanation: C.R.S. § 13-16-104 governs the allowance of costs in civil actions in Colorado. This section outlines that the prevailing party is entitled to recover their costs. The altered text states that while attorney's fees are recoverable, 'costs will be the responsibility of the party that hired the attorney' which effectively undermines the statute by preventing the prevailing party from recovering those associated costs as typically defined.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the typical understanding of cost recovery for the prevailing party in litigation. The original text clearly states that the prevailing party is entitled to 'all costs thereof including reasonable attorney's fees.' The changed text limits this by stating that 'costs will be the responsibility of the party that hired the attorney,' which directly opposes the original provision and undermines the prevailing party's ability to recover all costs as intended. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that this undermines the statute by preventing the prevailing party from recovering those associated costs as typically defined. While no specific law snippet was helpful, the law explanation specifically points to the standard of recovery of costs to the prevailing party. This significantly weakens the prevailing party's position and contradicts the original intent of cost recovery.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance without the laws of the State of Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance without the laws of the State of Colorado, except in cases of dispute, where the laws of the State of Delaware will apply.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a contradictory choice of law clause creates uncertainty as to which state's laws govern the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 and general conflict of law principles require a clear and consistent choice of law. Designating Colorado law, and then specifying Delaware law in case of dispute, creates an ambiguity and undermines the principle of a clear choice of law, making the contract's enforceability uncertain. It may be determined unenforceable in some instances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Conflict of laws - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Law for cases in multiple jurisdictions Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Conflict of laws (also called private international law ) is the set of rules or laws a jurisdiction applies to a case , transaction , or other occurrence that has connections to more than one jurisdiction. [ 1 ] This body of law deals with three broad topics: jurisdiction , rules regarding when it is appropriate for a court to hear such a case; foreign judgments , dealing with the rules by which a court in one jurisdiction mandates compliance with a ruling of a court in another jurisdiction; and choice of law , which addresses the question of which substantive laws will be applied in such a case. [ 2 ] These issues can arise in any private law context, [ 2 ] but they are especially prevalent in contract law [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] [ 9 ] [ 10 ] and tort law . [ 11 ] Scope and terminology [ edit ] The term conflict of laws is primarily used in the United States and Canada, though it has also come into use in the United Kingdom. Elsewhere, the term private international law is commonly used, [ 1 ] for example in Switzerland , the Federal Act on Private International Law (PILA) regulates which law should be applied when a dispute has connections with more than one jurisdiction. [ 12 ] Some scholars from countries that use conflict of laws consider the term private international law confusing because this body of law does not consist of laws that apply internationally, but rather is solely composed of domestic laws; the calculus only includes international law when the nation has treaty obligations (and even then, only to the extent that domestic law renders the treaty obligations enforceable). [ 13 ] The term private international law comes from the private law / public law dichotomy in civil law systems . [ 14 ] [ 15 ] In this form of legal system, the term private international law does not imply an agreed upon international legal corpus , but rather refers to those portions of domestic private law that apply to international issues. Importantly, while conflict of laws generally deals with disputes of an international nature, the applicable law itself is domestic law. This is because, unlike public international law (better known simply as international law ), conflict of laws does not regulate the relation between countries but rather how individual countries regulate internally the affairs of individuals with connections to more than one jurisdiction. To be sure, as in other contexts, domestic law can be affected by international treaties to which a country is party. Moreover, in federal states where substantial lawmaking occurs at the subnational level, notably in the United States, issues within conflict of laws often arise in wholly domestic contexts, relating to the laws of different states (or provinces, etc.) rather than of foreign countries. History [ edit ] Western legal systems first recognized a core underpinning of conflict of laws—namely, that "foreign law, in appropriate instances, should be applied to foreign cases"—in the twelfth century. [ 16 ] : 9–10 Prior to that, t
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by specifying that Delaware law will apply in case of a dispute, while also stating that Colorado law governs the agreement. This creates uncertainty and directly undermines the principle of a clear choice of law, as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions. By introducing Delaware law for disputes, the modified contract explicitly creates such a conflict, making it unclear which state's laws will ultimately govern the agreement's interpretation and enforcement. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' perturbation type because the contract uses conflicting state laws, deviating from the legal requirement of a consistent choice of law.

perturbation.original_text: ____________________ , agrees that, in consideration for being shown or told about certain trade secrets or property belonging to Navidec, Incorporated, ____________________, shall not disclose or cause to be disclosed, disseminated or distributed any information concerning said trade secret or property to any person, entity, business or other individual or company without the prior written permission of Navidec, Incorporated.
perturbation.changed_text: ____________________ , agrees that, in consideration for being shown or told about certain trade secrets or property belonging to Navidec, Incorporated, ____________________, shall not disclose or cause to be disclosed, disseminated or distributed any information concerning said trade secret or property to any person, entity, business or other individual or company without the prior written permission of Navidec, Incorporated, unless such disclosure is required by a valid subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: This allows disclosure under 'valid subpoena,' but many trade secret laws require attempts to protect trade secrets even when a subpoena is issued (e.g., notifying the trade secret holder).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act § 5. Notice of Intent to Disclose Trade Secret
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) itself does not directly address subpoenas, Section 5 requires that, upon application to the court, an order should be made to preserve the secrecy of an alleged trade secret. Allowing disclosure solely based on a subpoena without mandating prior notice or protective measures contradicts the underlying intention of trade secret protection, potentially waiving those rights by contract.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a loophole by allowing disclosure under a valid subpoena, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The Law Explanation highlights that Section 5 of the UTSA requires measures to preserve the secrecy of alleged trade secrets, implying that a simple subpoena is insufficient to justify disclosure without additional protective actions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a dead link. However, the core issue is that allowing disclosure solely based on a subpoena, without requiring any effort to protect the trade secret (like notifying the owner or seeking a protective order), contradicts the intent of trade secret law, creating a contradiction. The modification removes an obligation to protect trade secrets when a subpoena is issued.
